index,id: # MONOMORPHIZATION PHASE - Specialize polymorphic functions to concrete types

description: MonoType encodes fully elaborated runtime shapes so concrete Elm instantiations of source types appear as MInt MFloat MList MTuple MRecord MCustom or MFunction and the only remaining use of generic type variables is via MVar with an attached Constraint such as CEcoValue or CNumber that does not require further type inference
At MLIR codegen time no MonoType may contain MVar with CNumber constraint in any reachable position and any such occurrence indicates a failed specialization compiler bug
Remaining MVar with CEcoValue constraint are allowed and their concrete source types never affect runtime layout or calling convention and are tracked only for comparison or debugging purposes
Every MonoNode whose MonoType is a function must be callable so it is either MonoTailFunc params body monoType or MonoDefine expr monoType where expr is MonoClosure closureInfo body monoType

Each specialization identified by Global plus MonoType plus optional LambdaId has a unique SpecId recorded in SpecializationRegistry every MonoVarGlobal that mentions that SpecId refers to exactly one MonoNode implementing that specialization and there are no missing or orphan entries in the registry for any used instantiation
RecordLayout and TupleLayout store fieldCount indices and unboxedBitmap so field order indices and unboxing decisions for records and tuples are completely determined by these layouts
Record field access computes fieldIndex and isUnboxed from the record MonoType layout so MonoRecordAccess and MonoRecordUpdate always use indices and unboxing consistent with RecordLayout

Function call specialization unifies canonical function types with monomorphic argument types so primitive numeric types are fixed to MInt or MFloat and primitive mismatches are treated as monomorphization bugs

Polymorphic Debug kernel calls keep type variables as MVar by applying an empty substitution so their MonoType stays polymorphic and those type variables always have CEcoValue constraint and do not affect runtime layout or calling convention
MonoGraph is type complete and contains every type in the program including all aspects of those types such as all constructors for custom types

MonoGraph is closed and hygienic every MonoVarLocal refers to a binder in scope every MonoVarGlobal and SpecId refers to an existing MonoNode and there are no orphan specializations or unreachable SpecIds in the SpecializationRegistry

For every function or closure node the function MonoType arity matches its parameter list and closure information and all call sites supply arguments consistent with that MonoType so full and partial applications are always well formed

For each custom type every constructor has a CtorLayout whose field count ordering and unboxedBitmap match the constructor's fields and all construction and pattern matching nodes that use that constructor are consistent with its CtorLayout

Structurally equivalent record and tuple types share RecordLayout or TupleLayout identifiers or at least have layouts with identical indices and unboxedBitmap so layout metadata is canonical and does not spuriously duplicate equivalent layouts

index,id: # MLIR CODEGEN PHASE - Generate MLIR from monomorphized IR

description: MLIR codegen only inserts boxing and unboxing between primitive types and eco.value and any mismatch between different primitives such as i64 versus f64 is treated as a monomorphization bug rather than fixed locally
generateCall treats calls whose result MonoType is still a function as partial applications and routes them through closure generation and eco.papExtend instead of emitting direct calls

generateClosureApplication always boxes captured and applied arguments to eco.value uses remaining arity from the MonoType and emits eco.papExtend with operand_types listing eco.value for all operands

generateDestruct and generateMonoPath always use the destructor MonoType to determine the path target MLIR type and never use the body result type so destruct paths yield their natural type and do not spuriously unbox

generateMonoPath navigates containers as eco.value and uses eco.project with operand type eco.value and result type equal to the field type with the unboxed attribute set exactly when the result type is not eco.value
generateLet binds let names directly to the SSA value and MLIR type of the defining expression without wrapping in eco.construct so variable bindings preserve original boxed or unboxed representation

boxToMatchSignature and boxToMatchSignatureTyped adjust call arguments only for boxed versus unboxed differences so actual SSA types are made to match monoTypeToMlir of expected MonoTypes without changing primitive kinds
eco.construct eco.project eco.call and eco.return always carry an _operand_types attribute whose entries exactly match the MLIR SSA operand types used at that call site
Boolean constants must be emitted with typed MLIR attributes such as value equals 1 colon i1 so MLIR verifies that the constant value type and result type agree
Every eco.case in the IR has an explicit result_types attribute and all alternatives eco.return terminators match it

Every function referenced by eco.call must have a corresponding func.func definition or declaration in the module so no undefined function symbols escape MLIR codegen and the UndefinedFunction pass validates this invariant by failing the build on any violation

monoTypeToMlir maps primitive MonoTypes to their unboxed MLIR types so MInt becomes i64 and MFloat becomes f64 and MBool becomes i1 and MChar becomes i32 and all other MonoTypes including MVar and compound types become eco.value
At MLIR codegen time any MonoType component that is an MVar with CEcoValue constraint is always lowered to !eco.value so its concrete source type never affects generated layout or calling convention

index,id: # RUNTIME HEAP PHASE - Memory layout and GC invariants

description: Every heap object begins with an 8 byte Header struct and the first TAG_BITS bits of the header encode the Tag which denotes the concrete heap object kind
All heap objects are 8 byte aligned and getObjectSize always rounds object sizes up to a multiple of 8 bytes
GC and helper routines interpret heap object layout solely via Header.tag using a switch on the Tag enum in getObjectSize and related tracing functions
When introducing a new heap object type you must update the Tag enum the C plus plus struct definition getObjectSize scanObject and markChildren so all GC logic stays consistent with the Tag
There are no old to young pointers in the heap which is guaranteed by Elm immutability so the GC does not require a write barrier
Forwarding pointers use Tag_Forward and repurpose the header to store a logical pointer to the new location and they exist only during GC and never while mutator code runs
Each heap region is owned by exactly one ThreadLocalHeap so there are no cross thread heap pointers and each mutator collects only its own heap
All heap pointers are HPointer values whose ptr field is a 40 bit offset from heap_base so logical pointers are not raw addresses and only conversion helpers touch heap_base
HPointer is the canonical type for heap references and code must use fromPointer and toPointer or Allocator wrap and resolve rather than treating HPointer as a void star
Common constants such as Unit EmptyRec True False Nil Nothing and EmptyString are represented by nonzero HPointer.constant tags and never allocated as heap objects
Any allocation via ThreadLocalHeap allocate or Allocator allocate may trigger a minor GC which can move all live nursery objects so callers must assume heap locations may change across allocation
isInHeap uses an O(1) bounds check against heap_base plus heap_reserved so any pointer in the unified heap address range is treated as a potential heap object irrespective of generation

When allocating a heap object the Tag passed to Allocator allocate must correspond to the concrete struct written at that address because GC size and tracing logic derive layout solely from Header.tag
HPointer values with constant not equal to zero represent embedded constants and allocator and resolve operations must treat such pointers as non heap values that do not require tracing

For every heap allocation the header tag and in-memory layout must match the concrete runtime type such as Tuple2 Cons Record or Custom and no value of type List Tuple or Record is ever represented using the Custom struct because the tag and structure must always match

All eco_alloc_* runtime functions return uint64_t representing HPointer with constant field equal to 0 for heap objects and all functions receiving heap objects take uint64_t and use Allocator resolve internally to convert to raw pointers

Elm never produces null pointers so any allocation returning null indicates an out of memory condition and any HPointer with ptr equals 0 and constant equals 0 is a valid heap pointer to offset 0 not a null sentinel

Elm values are always acyclic because Elm is a pure functional language with immutable data and no recursive let bindings so any traversal of a value graph is guaranteed to terminate and runtime operations like debug printing may safely follow nested references without cycle detection

Container heap objects such as Custom Cons and Record store an unboxed bitmap in header.unboxed that indicates which value slots contain unboxed primitives rather than boxed HPointers and allocators must set this bitmap correctly at allocation time so that GC tracing and debug printing can distinguish unboxed primitive bits from boxed pointers

index,id: # CROSS-PHASE INVARIANTS - Constraints spanning multiple phases (always last)

description: RecordLayout TupleLayout and CtorLayout from the monomorphized IR must agree with eco.construct attributes tag size and unboxed_bitmap and with the Custom and Record C plus plus structs so that MLIR objects match runtime layouts
MLIR eco.value pointers and boxed values produced by codegen must correspond to HPointer based heap objects that satisfy the 8 byte alignment and header first invariants of the runtime heap
